speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, February 23, 1996, [? George ?] Kohn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being in a bakery, I was very happy. I was a businessman, and everything went fine. There was one day-- I used to get up, 5 o'clock to clean the-- feed the horses and prepare myself, and [? this and that. ?] And from nowhere, I see two girls, two women, wearing blankets on their heads. It was-- it was dark in the morning, you know. They saw a light in the stable, and they saw in the front of the building, [GERMAN]. You know. So they figured there must be bread here. So they walked in to the-- slowly to my stable. They opened the door, they said, anybody there, in Polish, you know. They were talking-- no in German they was talk to me-- they didn't know who I was. So I grabbed them, and I knew already. I knew this is it. I grabbed them, and  I took them into my stable. And I said, stay here for a minute. I didn't ask them no question. They didn't know I was-- who I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went upstairs-- I always had bread in my room. You know, baker, you know, private. So I brought out two breads, and I stuck them in. And I asked them, where are you coming from? Where are you going? In Polish, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, there's a whole group outside the village which the state brought us last night to keep them in the field. It was in spring in '44, '45, you know, '45-- in March, to keep them on the field, and nothing to eat. And they'd picking potato peels, whatever they can. And-- and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked them, where you-- where are you chasing-- where are you from? They told me, they chasing them-- they mentioned a place near [INAUDIBLE]. There was a camp-- [? Hassac ?] or something. You know. They liquidated the camp, and they'd taken deep Germany. They want to take them with-- with everybody. That probably they finished them, who knows? So anyway, I saw-- I heard that. They said, they got very nervous and very excited. And I said-- and I let them go. And I let them go, and I left. And-- and I made up my mind, I must go and see. And this is-- I know already-- I knew where it is, because it was a really small village. I crossed this place, was there a shoe maker. And-- and I figured, I can't go close to the field because I get spot and it's dangerous. So I took-- took a shoe, like boot, you know, on my-- on a bicycle. And my boss asked me, where you going? And I said, I have to go to the shoemaker because I'm going to the city, and I got to fix my shoe-- just an excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went into the shoemaker, and I looked out of the window, and I saw a terrible site. You know, so many women. Anyway, I went right back and from nowhere, the girls came back with the two bread-- probably they found out what's going on. Then the whole group came over-- it was daylight already. They came over to our place-- my boss was moseying around.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what's this? I didn't know what is this. Oh, this is Juden. You know, [INAUDIBLE]. And he calls me over. Bruno, come here, and tells me, come over, and he gives me-- a basket of bread was 25 in a basket, you know, like a straw basket-- we used to load up 25 bread each basket, and load up every morning. You know. So, he tells me, take a basket of bread and cut the bread in three, you know, and told me to cut in another basket. And lined up, there must have been 20 girls standing there. 20 girls came, and I don't know how they got out of there-- they would watch them with the-- with the machine guns and every thing. They could have sneaked out. They're a lot of-- and said, line them up, you in charge, give everybody. That was-- I couldn't forget it. I mean, he was-- he was a good man. So what happened, so I give every girl a portion. The one girl that said to him, lieber Herr, in Jewish-- in-- in-- in broken German, lieber Herr, can-- can I have another portion? Like in German, and said. He looks at me, and see, this is a Jude, this is a Jew. Give 'em a finger, he wants two. And that's a joke, I remember this. I can't forget it. So the girls left, and I made up. And I asked the girls-- I asked them, where are they taking you? Know where they're going to them? They don't know where. And I waited, and they went, probably they're gonna take us, but the minute we get back, they're going to chase us again. Anyway, I waited and waited, and I load up. I stretched it out as this much as I can, maybe-- they had to go by the bakery. Maybe I'll recognize something. Maybe I'll see something. So they-- we loaded up, and my boss asked me, [? no, ?] when are you going to sit? I said, not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, I see this. They're chasing them, machine guns, and they were-- they had little cans, they were begging for water. And they knocked the-- the cans out of-- with the machine guns, from both sides, German, chased them and chased them. And it took maybe a half an hour till they-- that went through the village, and the Germans watched. Somebody wanted to help. They'll knock them out. You know, the-- whatever they give them, they-- whatever they want to give them, they wouldn't let them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured I'm going to follow them. I let them-- I let them go on the highway. They went the same highway I went to the city. And I was ready to go, so I waited a while. They were walking. I was travelling. And I followed slowly, slowly, I followed the whole-- the whole group. And one woman-- whoever was behind, it didn't matter, they shot them. You know what I mean? They didn't wait. They didn't wait. But this woman, she had legs like this-- I'll never forget the site. I stopped the wagon, and I helped her up to my wagon-- it was a dangerous thing to do, you know, but I didn't care. I got up to the wagon, and I asked her-- what-- she couldn't walk any longer, her feet couldn't carry her-- so I asked, where are you from? She told me, a-- a place-- which my father used to buy shoes there-- Kielce, [? no, ?] she was from there-- and when we go through-- I don't know, we go to the left, another left [? another ?] left. So I had in my seat--

speaker: interviewee
text: I always had, not bread, I had rolls. You know. That's for me. You know. Rolls was a luxury. You know. Everything was rationed. I stuffed her up as much as I could. She was wearing in a blanket. I put in as much as I can. And I said, I'll take you as far as I can, but I have to let you go. While we are driving, she tells me stories, and stories, and it's in my mind, and I can't get over it. I had to-- I let her off before the city, and I don't know whatever happened-- what happened to her, I don't know if you found the group or not, and that's was the-- that was the time I knew already there are still some Jews left. And that's it. And afterwards-- so, if it came close-- the front was coming close-- we had a-- we had notices everywhere, every German home, household, farmers, we have to evacuate. Just-- just before the war, must be April sometimes. We must evacuate because the front is coming. And we don't-- we want you should get count in the-- in the Russian Army, with the Russian Army coming, occupying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was off again. My brother was away-- the [? Gourock ?] was a different city. It was a way, not far, maybe five miles, but I didn't see him. And I think he came to see me, he knew it, his village was also evacuated. He had to go with his boss, and I had to go with my boss. I had to drive to horses, and they took whatever, all of their valuable things, and we had to travel. So my brother came, I told him, look, remember, if you ever get separated, there's one place you find, is Wenzel. There's the place you could locate me or I'll locate you. And that was-- and I never saw him again until later after the war. So he went away in one direction. I went the other one. And we were traveling day and night until we got to-- to the River Elbe, it's called, near the Czech border. It's not-- wasn't far from the Czech border. And there-- there was the capitulation-- I mean, there was the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got there, so we found out all the Germans dropped the ammunition and everything, and they're surrounded. That was May the 8th. And my boss come-- so the minute I found out the war was over, so that was some celebration. And I think of my brother. And the-- and the Russian are approaching already, the Russian were not far, maybe a few miles away from us. And the next day, it was the 9th. May the 9th. So I'll go over, I said. And I-- I knew it's gonna be dangerous yet. So I had a bike, you know, my boss's bike, and I put a flag, a Polish flag, red and white, you know. This is I'm Polack, don't touch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went on the road to find-- maybe I find my brother. When I got on the highway, the Russian was sitting already in the-- in the-- in the side of the highway and they were celebrating, barrels of vodka. And they're drinking. And they pulled me in, let's celebrate, me too, and so they got me drunk. And I-- I got back to my boss, no brother, I couldn't find him and that's it. So my boss tells me, what are you gonna do now, Bruno? He asked me. I said, I'm going home. I'm going to Poland where I belong to. Maybe I find my parents. Maybe I find something.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'll give you half of my possession. You stay with me as long as you live. Whatever, it's-- it's yours. He was afraid for the Russian-- that's why he want to-- I said no, I'm not saying with nobody. I'm going back to my country. He didn't know I was Jewish anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got organized, and I went to greet-- greet the Russian, high officer. And I greeted him, and then you smiled, and I said, we are happy that you-- you freed us. And he didn't know I was Jewish anyway. So to go back, I had a problem. I have to go-- starting to head back to Poland. So the Russian-- that was a few weeks later-- they organized like your trucks, and they took back whatever they can, from the-- from the German, whatever they can, possessions. They load up, maybe 20 trucks, and they took it back to the Polish border, you know, to go through to Russia. So I approached one of those shaloms, you call it, the transport thing. I figured, this is the best way for me to go to cross the border, the Russian. So I was talking to an officer, said maybe you give me a lift. You know. I started to talk to the officer. I found out he's Jewish. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of officers in the army, the Russian. But, how did I spot him, I said oh, you're so happy, you know, and he spoke like a broken German. You know. He didn't know [INAUDIBLE]. A Polish-- I'm so happy that [INAUDIBLE], I said, [INAUDIBLE], this is not, this doesn't sound like, he was Jewish. And I said, hop in, I'll give you a lift. They put me up on top in the back of the truck. We were traveling maybe two days to the border. You know. They went through the Polish border, you know, the Polish-German border. And then I got out of the train, And we're traveling also another few days until I made up my mind, I'm going to Cmielow, back to my home town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arriving in-- after a few days, arriving in Cmielow, who do you think receives the-- the train? He was-- he was working-- he was a train master, Mr. Wenzel. He was in charge of the-- of receiving the trains and sending out the trains. So naturally, the train stopped on our station. So I get off the train, and whom do I see? Mr. Wenzel. He has a flag, you know, to flag down the train. He saw me, and he didn't recognize me after four years. I grew up. He looks at me-- who are you? I said, don't you remember me? He started hugged me and kissed me, and took the flag and threw it away. He says, enough, let me take you home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me home-- and I was so dirty from the-- from the smoke from the train. You know, it was so dirty. And he gave me meals and he-- and while we-- while being day, rolls out the couch-- you know, there was like a hide-a-bed. It was not a bed, it was a-- like a-- like an open space, he rolls it out, saying this is all yours. You take it. You're gonna need it. And, you know, the man, he was-- he was joyous. He was so happy, you know, to see me. And he tells me, you know, I wouldn't-- I wouldn't advise you to stay here. And one day comes-- I forgot the story, but he hid me in the attic. You know. That was the story in the paper, the attic. He kept me in the attic. And next door, when I with him the first time, the next-- next-- not far from him, a woman, Jewish boy went in to pick up laundry or something, and they-- they founded a boy, a Jewish boy was there. They took out the woman. They shot her right away on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neighbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: So what-- what this Wenzel said, don't stay here, and that time it happened that where they murdered 23 Jews in t you know, that was after the war. He said, it's very dangerous. They're killing Jews left and right-- not-- not the German, the Polish people. And I suggest you should leave as soon as you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all right, I took the train. I took the shoes. I went to Warsaw, and I went to the flea market, and they were selling the Jews-- the-- not the-- There was Jews already there, and I sold the shoes. And I went back to Lodz. I was told in Lodz, there's a lot of Jewish people. But I still had my identity-- my Polish identity I  didn't want to change. So I took the money, and went back to Lodz, and going into a building then was apartment buildings. Out the gate, you know, a Polack comes over-- I don't know whether he had a gun or not-- give me your money. So I had to give him all the money. And that was-- I left-- I left penniless again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, I found some-- a few-- a cousin of mine. He was in the army. He was in the Polish Army but in the Russian front. He was a uniform, and he said, you know, you come with me and we'll-- we'll go to Warsaw. We have friends. We're going to stay there until you contact your family, and then everything will be fine, but I haven't got my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't-- There was no phones there. There was nothing, and I-- and I gave Wenzel-- I told him I'm gonna contact you whenever I hear-- I hear from your-- my brother. And sure enough, my brother didn't show up until August. And that was already-- what was it? May, June. And we stayed in Warsaw-- not in Warsaw, Warsaw was all bunked out. But the outskirts, there was a-- a suburb of Warsaw. It was called-- I forget the name of the-- we stayed in a-- the family, they survived the war. And the apartment was theirs. So we spent some time there. And after that, I found my brother in-- in August, so we went-- we left Warsaw, and we went to Bratslav. Where was your brother now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's-- my brother's here.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I know, but at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, I found him, so we got together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he? In Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- we-- yeah, he came to Lodz. He found out from Wenzel where I went, so he looked for me and he found--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I came to Lodz, I wanted to turn back Jewish. I went in-- I had nothing to eat, so I had to get some provision from the UNRRA, from the Joint. You know, they set up kitchens and that. So I went to the Jewish Community to register. Said, you're not Jewish. You must be German. They wouldn't do it, because a lot of German converted to-- they wanted to covert, you know. They wanted to dress as Jews, because that was good. So they wouldn't. Anyway, I had a big problem, and finally they-- they discovered that I was Jewish. I couldn't speak Jewish. I forgot the language already. So they gave me a-- he gave me a provision and all that. And then my brother came to Lodz. And then we went-- from there, we went to Bratslav with my cousin. There was another captain from the army also. There's three of us-- four of us [? there. ?] We took over a store in Bratslav, and we run a store from the Germans. Everything was free. You know. And we had a-- we had our own business, you know. Yeah. It was not bad, but it wasn't-- it didn't last long. A few months later, they let out-- let out a rumor that-- that they're gonna-- they gonna make a pogrom in Bratslav. They're gonna-- a lot of Jews came back from Russia in that time. They settled in Bratslav. They said, they're going to kill all the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got frightened. We left the whole store in the middle of the night. My kid brother registered as a German. You know. He spoke good German-- they used to take the German-- they took the German back. They evacuated them from the East. You know, the Russian didn't want them. They shipped them back to-- to-- to the West, you know, German. So he went with the-- the transports, my kid brother. So I lost him again. So he ended up in Cologne. You know. And then afterwards, he came back. So we run away middle of the night-- left the store there-- and we crossed the border, the Czech border, middle of the night. And we went through all Czechoslovakia. And then we got into-- went into Vienna, and stayed a-- a-- a month, maybe, in Vienna. And then we went-- I ended up in Munich. And from Munich, I heard there was a-- a place in Stuttgart-- there was 1,500 Jews, made a camp there, [? [INAUDIBLE] ?] camp. So I went there, but I didn't stay and camp with the-- with the 1,500. Also, they shot maybe-- yeah, two Jews got shot in that camp, you know, from the German. So I-- I took a place, private. In order-- at that-- at that time, they give you-- for German-- if any Jew wanted to have quarters, they forced them to take you in. You know. So they gave me a room with a German family and I stayed there. And-- and afterwards, I-- I didn't know-- I lost my brother again. So I found out he-- he was in a camp not far from-- from Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart, I met that-- met that girl-- she was-- she survived in Stuttgart. She was a nurse under false identification, you know, like-- She survived. That's why I met the girl there. And she told me-- I told about her brother-- I met him in Munich. So-- so on-- so we were standing-- we were there in Stuttgart, and I couldn't do no business. Then I contact my family, I forgot. While in Stuttgart, we contact-- no before that, we contact our family. I know how I got-- I remembered an address. And finally they found that we're alive, and they tried to make papers for us to get us into Canada. But they warned me, don't smuggle, don't do nothing. Once you get caught, black market, so you could never come into Canada or anywhere. It was very dangerous. So we-- we suffered with it, and you had to eat what they gave us, you know, like provision. They had kitchens, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- so we-- I'm getting to it. So from Stuttgart-- so my kid brother worked in a camp, so he had all kind of bread he used to bring me, not far from was another camp, you know. So we-- the military-- we got the papers for my brothers, you know? So it took us maybe a year-- they had no passage. They had no transportation. There was no planes in those years, and no boats, but the one ship was going from Bremerhaven, from Germany. So we waited for the-- for that ship to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it left, I think, the ship left in-- in July, you know? So we got out of the ship, and we traveled maybe till end of August, August the 27th. We arrived in Canada at the port of Halifax.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers knew already that we're coming, and they took a small plane-- they came by plane. They were very wealthy, my brothers in Montreal. They had a big factory. The both of them came to greet us. It was-- the joy was out of this world. You know to-- we were the first ones to arrive in Montreal. There was no OTPs, nobody. Nobody was there yet because there was no passage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Halifax, they usually-- there was a lot of people came with us on the ship. But they had to go by train. But my brothers took me on a small plane, which took maybe four hours to-- to fly. But for the train, it would take 24 hours. It's a long trip to Montreal. So we got into Montreal. So right away, he set us up with the-- they rented for us a room, you know, a boarding room, you know, room and board, you know, for the two of us. And they wanted me to work for them in the factory, my brothers. So-- but I didn't want to work in factories. I said, I want to learn the language, English, and everything. So I got a job in a dry goods store, and I worked there for $20 a week. And there, she happened to work-- she was-- my wife was working in the office. She was young at the time. And there was-- I-- every day I greeted, spoke to her, said one day, well, I'll take you out. One Saturday-- Saturday night, I didn't work Saturday. So I took her out once, and then on Monday, she comes back with the stories that my father has a little business. He's looking for a partner-- maybe I know somebody from the-- from the [INAUDIBLE] maybe. He wants a partner. Said, a partner? I could be a partner. I wanted to go on business, you know. So I came to my brothers. I said, look, I have a good proposition. So my brothers said, let me see that business. It came in and said, this is a business? It's not enough even for-- for a half a person. He wanted, her father wanted-- half of the business was worth $7,000-- he wanted $2,500. So my brother didn't want to hurt me. You know. Said, well, it's not worth it, but what are we going to invest money, for what? I said, you invest the money. I promise I'll pay you back. That's what he did. They made a deal, and made a contract, and got-- I'm in business. So what happens, I'm in business. I had to come up to a house. So on and on, and so a few years-- no not so long, maybe a few months later, we got married. But I paid back my brother. In one year, I paid him back all the money, and I told my father-in-law, I told him this is not a business for you to build another business. So I started-- I got into the blouse business, and we moved out of the old place and got into a bigger place. And things were going not bad with enlarging the business. I paid up my brothers, and everything was fine. And when I got married-- so we went on our honeymoon. It was-- it wasn't the right time to go and wasn't the right place to go. We went to the Niagara Falls. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came back, so-- So we needed a place to live. In those years, you couldn't find an apartment, because you need key money and all that. We couldn't find a place, so we stayed with my in-laws. I said, this is not for me. I'm not going to stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave us a room-- her room, you know. We stayed there. But I went out one day. I saw this project, and I called my wife. I called her-- she was still working in the same office, you know-- and I call her. Guess what? And I went in [? on ?] this project. I bought a house. She said, what? You bought a house? With what? That's all I have to give. I had to give a signature. I had to give him $250 a down payment. It wasn't built yet. And we got the house. You know. So it was-- they were surprised, you know? And it was a duplex, you know, a two-family house. So we moved in. And we convinced her parents to take the other half. You know. I wouldn't take money from them. You pay me out-- I had no money myself-- so I said with what we going to buy? I'm paying rent $25 a month. They were happy, so either way we got them in. The idea was that we should have them next to us. Kids will come. Oh we're finished? OK.
